question_title: Will the U.S. Supreme Court docket for Danco Laboratories v. Louisiana (No. 25A1207) show any new docket entry dated between May 18, 2026 and May 30, 2026 (inclusive)?
summary: SCOTUS emergency stay A-dockets go dormant after the stay is granted. In all 4 comparable cases examined (including the prior mifepristone case 22A902), zero post-stay entries appeared within the first month. The stay in 25A1207 was granted May 14; the case is now procedurally at the Fifth Circuit with no pending SCOTUS activity. No news indicates any party plans to file motions or letters on this docket. The 15% YES probability reflects the Bayesian uncertainty from a small sample (0/4), the low qualifying threshold (any Proceedings and Orders entry counts), the many parties involved, and the 13-day window. The most likely NO scenario is simply continued dormancy - the normal pattern. The rare YES scenario would require an unexpected motion (to vacate/modify the stay), a supplemental authority letter prompted by a new legal development, or some minor administrative filing.

factors.description: Base rate from comparable SCOTUS A-dockets: 0 out of 4 comparable cases had ANY post-stay-grant entries within first month (22A902, 24A910, 24A966, 23A470). Bayesian estimate with uninformative prior gives ~13-17% mean.

Direct comparable: 22A902 (prior mifepristone stay, April 2023) had ZERO post-stay entries despite identical profile (high-profile, many amici, politically charged). Most informative single datapoint.
No news or indications of any pending filings, motions to vacate, or supplemental authority letters. No party appears to be planning activity on this specific docket.
Case is procedurally at the Fifth Circuit now. All action moves there. SCOTUS A-docket has no pending briefing, no scheduled oral argument, no motion awaiting decision.

High-profile case with 40+ amici and many parties slightly increases probability of some minor administrative filing (late amicus processing, counsel withdrawal, supplemental letter). Low threshold - ANY entry in Proceedings and Orders table counts.
13-day window (May 18-30) gives reasonable opportunity for something to happen. Two full business weeks.

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary: **15% YES** (85% NO)

**Question**: Will the SCOTUS docket for Danco Laboratories v. Louisiana (25A1207) show any new entry dated May 18-30, 2026?

### Key Findings

**Base Rate**: I examined 4 comparable SCOTUS emergency stay A-dockets after the stay was granted:
| Case | Stay Granted | Post-Stay Entries (within ~1 month) |
|------|-------------|--------------------------------------|
| 22A902 (Mifepristone 2023) | Apr 21, 2023 | **ZERO** |
| 24A910 (Dept of Ed) | Apr 4, 2025 | **ZERO** |
| 24A966 (NLRB) | May 22, 2025 | **ZERO** |
| 23A470 (Idaho EMTALA) | Jan 5, 2024 | **ZERO** (one entry 6 months later) |

**All four comparables had zero post-stay entries** in the weeks following the stay grant. The most informative comparable is 22A902 — the *prior* mifepristone case — which had identical characteristics (high-profile, many amici, politically charged) and zero post-stay activity.

### Why 15% and not lower?
- Small sample (only 4 cases) creates genuine uncertainty about the true base rate
- Extremely low qualifying threshold — ANY entry in the Proceedings and Orders table counts
- Many parties and 40+ amici create larger surface area for minor filings
- 13-day window is two full business weeks

### Most likely scenario (85%): NO
The docket remains dormant as the case proceeds at the Fifth Circuit, following the standard pattern for post-stay A-dockets.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the U.S. Supreme Court docket for Danco Laboratories v. Louisiana (No. 25A1207) show any new docket entry dated between May 18, 2026 and May 30, 2026 (inclusive)?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43532)
resolution_criteria:
This question resolves YES if, as of 11:59 p.m. ET on May 30, 2026, the public Supreme Court docket for No. 25A1207 at https://www.supremecourt.gov/docket/docketfiles/html/public/25a1207.html displays at least one entry in the 'Proceedings and Orders' table with a date on or after May 18, 2026 and on or before May 30, 2026. Otherwise, it resolves NO.

Any entry counts (orders, motions, letters, amicus filings, notices of appearance, withdrawal of counsel, etc.) so long as it appears as a row in the docket's Proceedings and Orders table with a date in that range. The date of the entry, as listed on the docket, is what controls — not the date a document may have been signed or executed elsewhere.

If the supremecourt.gov docket page is unreachable on May 30–31, 2026, the resolver may consult the equivalent docket mirror at https://www.law.cornell.edu/supremecourt/text/25A1207 or any archived snapshot from web.archive.org of the supremecourt.gov docket page captured on or after May 30, 2026 to determine the answer.
fine_print:
'Proceedings and Orders' refers to the chronological table of dated docket entries on the SCOTUS docket page (the same section that currently lists entries from May 2–14, 2026). Entries in the counsel/attorneys section, the lower court metadata, or the case caption do not count; only dated rows in the Proceedings and Orders table count.

If the Court relists, consolidates, redockets, or transfers the matter to a new docket number, that does not by itself create a qualifying entry on 25A1207 unless a corresponding dated row also appears on the 25A1207 docket page in the date range.

Direct comparable: 22A902 (prior mifepristone stay, April 2023) had ZERO post-stay entries despite similar profile (high-profile, many amici, politically charged). This is the most informative single datapoint.
No news or indications of any pending filings, motions to vacate, or supplemental authority letters. No party appears to be planning activity on this specific docket.

High-profile case with 40+ amici and many parties slightly increases probability of some minor administrative filing (late amicus processing, counsel withdrawal, supplemental letter). Low threshold - ANY entry counts.
13-day window (May 18-30) is a reasonably long period, giving more opportunity for something to happen vs a shorter window.

input.anchor: Base rate from 4 comparable SCOTUS emergency stay A-dockets: 0/4 had post-stay entries within ~1 month. Bayesian Beta(1,5) mean = 17%, median = 13%.
input.assessment:
This question asks whether ANY new entry will appear on SCOTUS docket 25A1207 between May 18-30, 2026. The stay was granted May 14, and the docket has been dormant since.

The empirical evidence is quite clear: A-dockets go dormant after stay grants. I examined 4 comparable cases (22A902 mifepristone 2023, 24A910 Dept of Ed, 24A966 NLRB, 23A470 Idaho EMTALA) and found ZERO had any entries within the first month after the stay was granted. The most informative comparable is 22A902, which is literally the prior iteration of this same mifepristone controversy - it had zero post-stay entries despite similar high-profile nature and many amici.

The case is now procedurally at the Fifth Circuit. All substantive action moves there. There's no pending briefing, no scheduled hearing, no motion awaiting decision at SCOTUS. The only things that could create a qualifying entry are:
1. A party filing a motion to vacate/modify the stay (unlikely without changed circumstances)
2. A supplemental authority letter (rare; requires relevant new legal development)
3. Late-processed amicus filing (unlikely - all were processed May 4-8)
4. Counsel withdrawal/substitution (possible with many attorneys but would typically go in counsel section, not Proceedings and Orders)
5. Some administrative or clerical entry

The resolution criteria specify only entries in the 'Proceedings and Orders' table count - not counsel section changes. This narrows the qualifying events further.

Against the NO case: the question was published today, suggesting the question author may have reason to think something could happen. The case is exceptionally politically charged. With many parties and counsel, minor filings are not impossible. But none of these rise above mild evidence.

My estimate of ~17% YES reflects the Bayesian uncertainty from a small sample (0/4) plus a slight upward adjustment for the many parties and political stakes, offset by the strong signal from the most direct comparable.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor is 13-17% from Bayesian base rate (0/4 comparables). My tentative estimate of 17% sits at the upper end, reflecting the case's exceptional profile. Hedging check: 17% is not near 50%, so no hedging concern. This is a clear lean toward NO based on strong empirical evidence.
input.key_uncertainties:
1. Small sample of comparables (only 4 cases examined) - if the true base rate is higher than 0/4 suggests, the probability could be 25-30%.
2. Whether any party is planning an unexpected filing that wouldn't show up in news searches.
3. Whether the political dynamics of this case create unprecedented SCOTUS docket activity.
4. Whether the SCOTUS clerk processes any late or corrective entry in this window.
input.tool_audit:
Useful: fetch_url (directly read the SCOTUS docket and 4 comparable dockets - critical for this question), web_search (found relevant news coverage and comparable cases), research() (good background on SCOTUS A-docket patterns and case details).

Empty but informative: web search for pending filings/motions returned nothing, which is itself evidence for NO.

No failures encountered.
input.update_triggers:
- News of any party announcing plans to file a motion at SCOTUS
- Louisiana filing a motion to vacate or modify the stay
- A major legal or political development related to mifepristone (Congressional action, FDA action)
- Any entry appearing on the companion docket 25A1208 (would suggest similar activity on 25A1207)
input.process_reflection: This question was well-suited to the tools available. Direct docket fetching was the key capability - being able to read the actual SCOTUS docket pages and comparable cases provided the core evidence. The research() tool gave good background on A-docket patterns. The main limitation was the small number of comparables I could examine - I checked 4-5 cases but ideally would have checked 15-20 to get a more robust base rate. A specialized SCOTUS docket search tool or a legal research database (Westlaw, PACER) would have helped identify more comparables systematically.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**Resolution criteria**: Fully provided and correctly engaged. The agent specifically addressed the "Proceedings and Orders" table requirement, the date range (May 18-30), the specific docket URL, and the fine print about counsel section entries not counting.

**Factor verification against trace**:

- **Factor 1 (Base rate 0/4 comparable cases)**: All four comparable dockets were directly fetched via fetch_url. 22A902 confirmed zero entries after April 21, 2023; 24A910 confirmed zero after April 4, 2025; 24A966 confirmed zero after May 22, 2025; 23A470 had one entry 6 months later (stay vacated), correctly excluded from "within first month" count. Bayesian computation (Beta posteriors) confirmed in execute_code output. **Grounded**.

- **Factor 2 (22A902 as most informative comparable)**: The trace confirms 22A902 is the prior mifepristone case with zero post-stay entries. **Grounded**.

- **Factor 3 (No news of pending filings)**: Web search for "motion to vacate" / "modify stay" / "filing" returned no results suggesting planned activity on this docket. **Grounded by absence of contradicting evidence**.

- **Factor 4 (Case at Fifth Circuit)**: Confirmed by the docket showing stay granted "pending disposition of the appeal" in the Fifth Circuit. **Grounded**.

- **Factor 5 (High-profile case, many amici)**: 40+ amicus briefs visible on docket fetches for both 25A1207 and 25A1208. **Grounded**.

- **Factor 6 (13-day window)**: Simple arithmetic, correct. **Grounded**.

**Double-counting**: Factors 1, 2, and 4 share overlapping evidence — the base rate from A-dockets (Factor 1) includes 22A902 (Factor 2), and the reason dockets go dormant (Factor 1) is the same mechanism as the case being at the Fifth Circuit (Factor 4). Combined logit of -3.0 from these three factors alone would push the implied probability to ~5%. However, the agent explicitly recognized this overlap in the reflection phase ("some of the negative factors are partially redundant") and adjusted the final probability upward from the factor-implied 7% to 17%. This self-correction adequately addresses the double-counting.

**Wrong-direction factors**: None found. All logit signs match their descriptions.

**Contradictory assessment**: The assessment (17% YES) is consistent with the factor direction (strongly NO-leaning, with mild upward adjustments).

**Counterargument quality**: Strong — identifies small sample fallacy, the extremely low "any entry" threshold, specific trigger scenarios, and provides concrete evidence thresholds that would change the forecast. Not a token gesture.

**Self-assessment calibration**: 0.45 confidence is appropriate given the 4-case sample, wide Bayesian CI (1%-45%), and acknowledged uncertainty.

**Probability assessment**: The 17% estimate falls at the top of the Bayesian range (7.7%-16.7% depending on prior) with a slight upward adjustment for the many parties and low threshold. My independent estimate based on the same evidence would be 10-18%. The agent's 17% is at the upper end but defensible given the uninformative prior and genuine uncertainty. No meaningful divergence.

**Anchor divergence**: None — the anchor (Beta(1,5) mean = 17%) matches the final probability exactly.

**Worldview consistency**: No conflicting forecasts or research entries found in the worldview store. The two research entries created during this session (SCOTUS A-docket patterns and Danco case details) are consistent with the factors.

**Tool audit**: Agent claims 0 errors across 17 calls, consistent with ground truth (0.0% error rate). No discrepancy.

**25A312 exclusion**: The agent correctly excluded Trump v. Cook (25A312) from the base rate as a "special case — deferred pending oral argument," recognizing that conversion to merits is a different procedural posture from a simple stay grant. Sound judgment.

No hallucinated evidence, no wrong-direction factors, no contradictory assessment, no missing resolution criteria, no meaningful probability divergence. The mild double-counting in factors 1/2/4 was self-identified and corrected.
